judge.justification: A is slightly better overall. On skill_accuracy, A preserves the MCP tool from the resume's skills section which B omits — a real demonstrated skill. On conservatism, A's skill groupings are more precise (separating Frontend from Backend technologies) and better calibrated to the evidence. Both outputs capture concrete metrics faithfully (1,000+ orders, 52% latency reduction, 98% time reduction, ~5 hrs/week saved) giving equal impact_quality scores. Project classification is equivalent across both — neither output uses the taxonomy labels explicitly, but both correctly identify the nature of each project. The github/linkedin URLs in B are truncated/guessed ('sujan' vs the more complete attempt in A), which is a minor accuracy issue. Overall A is the cleaner, more complete extraction.

Both correctly extract the same skill set (including Scikit-Learn from demonstrated project use), maintain appropriate conservatism, omit explicit project_type classification fields (making both neutral on that dimension), and capture the same concrete metrics (52% latency reduction, 98% time reduction, 83% accuracy, 5 hrs/week saved, 1,000+ orders, 30,000+ students). Neither difference is significant enough to declare a clear winner — the outputs are genuinely equivalent within one point on every dimension.

judge.justification: Both extractions are nearly identical in quality across all four dimensions. Both accurately capture skills demonstrated in the resume without hallucination, extract a well-calibrated set of skills, and faithfully preserve all concrete metrics (83% code reduction, 52% latency reduction, 98% time reduction, 5 hrs/week saved, 1,000+ orders, etc.). Neither output includes explicit project type classifications per the taxonomy, so both score neutral on project_classification. The primary differences are minor: B preserves the original resume language more faithfully (less embellishment like 'intelligent API design'), B includes coursework as a skill category (a minor plus for completeness), and A uses slightly more inflated language ('Architected', 'Engineered') that borders on embellishment. These differences are within the 0-1 point threshold for a tie across all dimensions.

judge.justification: A preserves all concrete metrics faithfully and verbatim where appropriate (1,000+ orders, 83% code reduction, 30,000+ students, 52% latency reduction, 98% time reduction, sub-10ms WebSocket), which is exactly what the expected behavior calls for. B paraphrases these metrics but occasionally softens or obscures them (e.g., 'reducing code complexity by 83%' vs the cleaner '83% code reduction (30+ lines to 5)'). A also retains AI-specific skills like LangChain, RAG, Prompt Engineering, Vector Stores, and MCP — all genuinely demonstrated in the resume — whereas B drops the entire AI Tools category from its skills section, which is a significant omission for a candidate whose strongest work is AI/LLM-driven. B's github and linkedin fields contain placeholder-style shortened URLs rather than real ones, reducing data fidelity. Both outputs classify projects similarly (Full-Stack Web App with AI components), so project_classification is roughly equal. A is clearly superior on skill completeness and impact metric preservation.

Architected end-to-end ML workflow automating lead qualification, debounce logic, and calendar booking for qualified prospects with production monitoring and error handling

projects.name: Internship Matcher (React + TypeScript, FastAPI, Claude, PostgreSQL, Redis, AWS S3)
bullets: Owned end-to-end at internshipmatcher.com (live in production): React + TypeScript frontend, FastAPI backend, multi-tier data layer (PostgreSQL, Redis, AWS S3), and Clerk JWT auth via JWKS

Designed dual-model Claude pipeline (Haiku 4.5 for fast skill extraction, Sonnet 4.5 for deep re-ranking of top 30 to top 10) at 95%+ accuracy; SSE-streamed progress events to the React frontend in real time
Parallel processing 30+ positions, 52% latency reduction (270s to 130s) via dynamic batching and prompt caching

projects.name: Sleep-PSG Scoring (React, FastAPI, MNE, Scikit-Learn, Redis, WebSockets)
bullets: Built automated PSG sleep-stage scoring platform with 98% time reduction (2+ hrs to under 2 min) and 83% balanced accuracy, validated against clinician-labeled hypnograms from the Sleep-EDF dataset
Async EEG pipeline (Redis + ARQ): distributed EDF parsing, spectral features, Scikit-Learn classifiers per patient
Sub-10ms WebSocket streaming to React dashboard rendering live EEG waveforms and hypnograms for QA review

projects.name: Cold Leads Agent (FastAPI, Claude Haiku + Sonnet, Cal.com, Linq, Postgres)
bullets: Built and deployed an autonomous lead-qualification SMS agent for a Bay Area real estate client: FSM-driven pipeline with intent classification, debounce sweeper, and Cal.com auto-booking of qualified leads
Multi-service integration: HMAC-signed Linq SMS webhooks, Cal.com v2 booking, SendGrid escalation; dual-model Claude (Haiku + Sonnet) on async Postgres with Alembic migrations and APScheduler
Live in production for the client, auto-booking qualified leads on Cal.com and saving ~5 hrs/week of manual outreach

judge.justification: A faithfully reproduces the resume's own language and metrics without embellishment, preserving exact numbers (83% code reduction from 30+ lines to 5, 52% latency reduction 270s→130s, 98% time reduction, sub-10ms WebSocket, ~5 hrs/week) and deployment evidence (internshipmatcher.com live in production). B rewrites bullets with data-engineering framing ('scalable data infrastructure', 'data platform reliability', 'distributed data pipeline', 'ML inference pipeline') that inflates the data-engineering angle beyond what the resume demonstrates — exactly the fabrication risk flagged in the expected behavior. B also adds Scikit-Learn and MNE to the skills section, which are legitimate but push the list slightly beyond the core demonstrated set, and its GitHub/LinkedIn URLs are truncated/guessed. A's skills section is clean and directly mirrors the resume's own Technical Skills section. On impact quality, A preserves every concrete metric verbatim while B occasionally softens or reframes them. Both classify projects reasonably (Full-Stack/AI-ML hybrids), so project_classification is roughly equal. Overall A is more accurate, conservative, and faithful to the source.

judge.justification: Both outputs are very close across all dimensions — accurate skills, well-calibrated counts, no project type taxonomy applied (neutral score for both), and strong concrete metrics preserved. The tiebreaker goes to B for two reasons: (1) B preserves the original resume bullets more faithfully without paraphrasing that risks distortion (e.g., A introduces 'production multi-channel B2B AI agent infrastructure' and 'configuration integrity' language not in the source), and (2) B correctly uses the website-matching GitHub handle 'suqjan' consistent with the personal site 'suqjan.com', while A fabricates 'github.com/sujan'. Truthfulness and fidelity to source text — the core stress-test criterion — favor B.
